sections.heading: What is a Noun?
sections.body:
A noun is a word that names a person, place, thing, or idea.

Person: teacher, doctor, Ali, Sarah
Place: school, city, London, kitchen
Thing: book, phone, car, computer
Idea: love, freedom, happiness, truth

Types of nouns:
• Common noun: teacher (general)
• Proper noun: Mr. Khan (specific — always capitalized)
• Countable: books, chairs, ideas
• Uncountable: water, rice, information

sections.heading: What is a Pronoun?
sections.body:
A pronoun replaces a noun to avoid repetition.

Instead of: "Ali is a student. Ali studies hard."
Write: "Ali is a student. He studies hard."

Personal pronouns:
• I, you, he, she, it, we, they (subject)
• me, you, him, her, it, us, them (object)
• my, your, his, her, its, our, their (possessive)

sections.heading: Articles: a, an, the
sections.body:
"A" before consonant sounds: a book, a car, a university (sounds like "you")
"An" before vowel sounds: an apple, an egg, an hour (silent h)
"The" for specific things: the book on the table, the sun

No article for general plural: Dogs are loyal.
The article for specific plural: The dogs in the park are friendly.

jargon.definition: A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea. Examples: book, London, happiness, teacher.
A word used in place of a noun. Examples: he, she, it, they, we. Prevents repetition.
A small word placed before a noun: "a" (indefinite, one of many), "an" (before vowel sounds), "the" (definite, specific).
Nouns that can be counted: one book, two books. They have singular and plural forms.
Nouns that cannot be counted individually: water, information, advice. No plural form, use "much" not "many".

summary: Every English sentence needs nouns and usually pronouns. Master articles early — "a/an/the" mistakes are the most common for non-native speakers.
